title: DIY Mudroom System with Towers and Bench
summary: Two frameless storage towers plus a matching bench, all built from plywood strips and dressed up with real base moulding — a full mudroom wall in one project.
description:
Three separate plywood boxes — two tall towers and a bench — designed to sit together as one built-in-looking wall unit, even though nothing about the construction requires them to be permanently joined. Each tower is frameless (no applied face frame), which means the back panel is doing real structural work here, not just closing up the back: without it, a frameless box like this can rack out of square the first time someone leans on a door or overloads a shelf.

Base moulding along the bottom and edge moulding on the bench are what elevate this from "plywood boxes" to something that reads as built-in furniture — skip them and it still functions, but skip them at your own risk if the goal is a finished look. The bench doors are optional; a plain center divider with no doors is a legitimate simpler finish if hanging doors and hardware isn't where you want to spend your time on this build.

tools.note: For checking the towers and bench are square and true, especially if securing to a wall.

materials.name: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Have it ripped into 15-3/4" wide, 8 ft long strips at the store.

cutList.note: Space at least one center partition — a bench this wide sags without it.
Optional — cut to fit the actual opening.

steps.title: Build the two towers
steps.body: Build each tower's sides, shelves, and cubby dividers with 3/4" pocket holes and glue. Include at least one fixed shelf near the center of each tower — a fixed shelf keeps a tall frameless tower rigid even if the rest of the shelves are set on adjustable shelf pins.
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Add the shoe cubbies
steps.body: Build the plus-shaped cubby dividers as their own small assembly and slide it into place inside each tower — building it separately and sliding it in makes it removable later if you want to reconfigure the shelving.
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Attach the tower backs
steps.body: Attach a back panel to each tower. Backing a frameless tower isn't optional here — it's what keeps the whole thing square once it's loaded with weight and someone starts hanging on the doors or shelves.

steps.title: Build the bench box
steps.body: Build the bench box with pocket holes, 2" screws, or nails — whichever hardware you used for the towers. Don't build it any wider than planned without adding a center partition; an unsupported bench top this size will sag under sitting weight over time.
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Back the bench
steps.body: Add a back panel to the bench, same as the towers — it's just as important here for keeping the box square.

steps.title: Add base moulding
steps.body: Attach base moulding along the bottom of both towers and the bench. Edge moulding on the bench top's front edge (rather than edge banding) holds up better in this high-wear spot where people sit down and stand up constantly.

steps.title: Add doors or a center divider
steps.body: If you want doors, cut leftover 3/4" stock down to size and fit into the bench opening with an even 1/8" gap all around. Dress up the door edges with a strip of decorative 1/4" x 2-1/2" hobby stock and add a knob. If doors feel like more than you want to take on, a simple center divider nailed and glued in place is a perfectly good alternative.

images.alt: Mudroom storage system with two towers and a matching bench
